name: Borr Drilling Limited

profile.description: Borr Drilling Limited is an international offshore drilling contractor that owns and operates a fleet of modern jack-up rigs, one of the youngest fleets in the industry. Founded in 2016 and headquartered in Hamilton, Bermuda, the company provides drilling services to oil and gas exploration and production companies worldwide in water depths up to approximately 400 feet, with operations concentrated in the Middle East, Southeast Asia, West Africa, and Mexico. Fleet expanded to approximately 34 rigs following the $360M Noble Corporation acquisition in January 2026.

innovation.label: WEAK
innovation.analysis: Track Record (1/3): Borr completed a $360M acquisition of five premium jack-up rigs from Noble Corporation in January 2026, expanding its fleet significantly, but this is asset consolidation rather than product innovation or business model transformation [1]. No new drilling technologies or service line innovations in the past five years. AI Deployment (0/2): No evidence of AI-powered products or quantified AI-driven operational savings. The offshore drilling industry remains heavily equipment- and crew-driven, and Borr has not disclosed AI deployments despite peers like Chevron reporting 30% drilling speed improvements from AI [2]. Market Share (1/2): Borr operates one of the youngest premium jack-up fleets globally with 97.9% technical utilization, and the Noble acquisition strengthens its competitive position, though it remains behind Valaris and ADES in total fleet size [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): Noble acquisition adds five premium rigs with 2026 contract coverage at 80% in H1 and 48% in H2, providing near-term revenue visibility, but no next-generation technology platforms or new service lines have been announced [1]. R&D Intensity (0/1): Minimal R&D spending as an asset-heavy drilling services company; innovation is driven by rig specification and operational efficiency rather than proprietary technology development.

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (1/4): Borr's vision 'To be the Leading Offshore Drilling Company' is a generic market position aspiration interchangeable with any drilling peer — no articulation of deeper purpose or why the company exists beyond shareholder returns and operational excellence [3]. Community Impact (1/2): The company commits to recruiting local talent in operating regions and references ecosystem and community well-being in sustainability disclosures, with QHSE culture emphasized, though no specific dollar amounts or distinctive community investment programs are cited [3]. Employee Retention (0/2): Glassdoor rating of 3.1/5 with only 41% recommending the company to a friend; reviews cite 'fear and micromanagement' in HR culture, 'lots of politics and back stabbing,' and concerns about senior leadership — well below industry average [4]. Insider Buying (1/2): Major shareholder Thiago Mordehachvili holds over 19% ownership and was appointed to the board, demonstrating significant insider alignment, though this is a legacy concentrated position rather than recent open-market purchases [2].

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. Stock has declined 30% from $6.05 to $4.24 since March 2026, reflecting market concern over FY2026 EPS guidance of -$0.11 [5]. Q1 2026 revenue of $247M and full-year guidance of $1.06B represent modest 4% growth from $1.02B in 2025, while TTM Adjusted EBITDA of ~$470M demonstrates operating leverage [1]. Net income margin of 3.13% is razor-thin due to $2.31B in debt against $246M cash, yielding D/E of 193% — well above the 70% target [5]. ROE of 3.03% is anemic. Forward P/E of 173x is essentially uninvestable at current earnings levels. The five-year revenue CAGR of ~43% from 2021-2025 is exceptional, driven by post-COVID offshore recovery and fleet expansion. Altman Z-Score of 0.37 signals elevated bankruptcy risk [5]. Liquidity floor cleared: 8.5M avg vol x $4.24 = $36.1M.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Offshore jack-up drilling is a physical-world operation requiring massive capital equipment ($50M+ per rig), specialized crews, regulatory permitting, and real-time subsea engineering that AI cannot substitute [1][3]. The core revenue model — deploying rigs under multi-year dayrate contracts — involves physical asset utilization in harsh marine environments where human judgment and mechanical execution are irreplaceable. AI could modestly improve drilling efficiency through predictive maintenance and wellbore optimization, and peers like Chevron report 30% drilling speed increases from AI, but this enhances rather than threatens the drilling contractor business model [2]. AI Opportunity (0/1): No evidence of AI-generated revenue. Borr has not announced AI-powered services or products generating measurable revenue despite the broader industry adopting AI for drilling optimization.

infinite_game.summary: Borr Drilling operates with a primarily finite mindset focused on fleet expansion, market share acquisition, and shareholder returns rather than an enduring purpose. The company was founded in 2016 as a financial play to acquire modern jack-up rigs at distressed prices during the offshore downturn — a shrewd financial strategy but not an infinite game cause. The recent CEO transition from Patrick Schorn to Bruno Morand and the aggressive $360M Noble acquisition reflect a company optimizing for near-term market positioning rather than building something designed to endure across energy transitions.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (0/3): Borr's vision 'To be the Leading Offshore Drilling Company' is a market position goal, not a Just Cause [3]. The company was founded in 2016 specifically to exploit distressed jack-up asset prices during the offshore downturn — a financially motivated founding rather than a purpose-driven one. There is no articulated vision of a better future that would qualify as resilient, inclusive, service-oriented, idealistic, or big under Sinek's framework. The company exists to generate returns by operating drilling rigs efficiently.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): The $360M Noble rig acquisition in January 2026, funded through additional debt in a potentially softening dayrate environment, demonstrates willingness to invest counter-cyclically [1][2]. Building a major drilling fleet from scratch during the 2016-2020 offshore downturn required conviction in the cycle turn. However, these decisions are financially motivated (buying cheap assets in a down cycle) rather than principled long-term bets that sacrifice near-term results for an enduring cause.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): Glassdoor rating of 3.1/5 with only 41% of employees recommending the company is a significant red flag [4]. Reviews cite 'fear and micromanagement' in HR culture, 'lots of politics and back stabbing,' and concerns about senior leadership. This suggests a low-trust environment where employees spend energy on self-protection. The September 2025 CEO transition from Patrick Schorn to Bruno Morand (with Schorn moving to Executive Chairman) adds organizational uncertainty.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): Borr has shown some strategic flexibility by adding Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) drilling capabilities and diversifying geographically into Mexico, the Middle East, West Africa, and Southeast Asia [3]. Growing from zero rigs in 2016 to ~34 rigs in 2026 through well-timed acquisitions demonstrates strategic ambition. However, the company remains entirely within jack-up drilling — no diversification into deepwater, renewable energy, or adjacent services.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Borr's competitive language focuses on becoming the 'leading' offshore drilling company and gaining market share through fleet modernization and scale [3]. The Noble acquisition was framed as a consolidation play for competitive advantage. No evidence of management referencing Valaris, ADES, or other competitors' strengths as motivation for self-improvement.

investment_thesis: Borr Drilling operates one of the youngest and highest-specification jack-up fleets in the industry (~34 rigs post-Noble acquisition) in a structurally undersupplied market — limited new rig orders since 2015 mean existing modern fleets command premium dayrates [1]. Q1 2026 revenue of $247M and full-year guidance of $1.06B demonstrate steady operational momentum, with Adjusted EBITDA margins near 35% and H1 2026 contract coverage at 80% [1]. The analyst consensus price target of $5.71 implies 35% upside from $4.24, and the five-year revenue trajectory from $245M (2021) to $1.06B (2026E) reflects successful fleet deployment. Saudi Arabia and Mexico show signs of jack-up demand inflection into 2027 [2].
thesis_continued: The bear case is dominated by balance sheet risk and earnings deterioration. With $2.31B in debt against $246M cash, D/E of 193%, and an Altman Z-Score of 0.37, Borr faces material financial distress risk in a prolonged dayrate downturn [5]. FY2026 consensus EPS of -$0.11 suggests the company may not cover interest costs, and profit margin of 3.13% means even modest dayrate softness pushes into sustained losses. The stock has declined 30% since March 2026, reflecting market skepticism — 9.57% short interest underscores the bearish thesis [5]. H2 2026 contract coverage of only 48% leaves significant revenue uncertainty. The energy transition poses a secular headwind as ESG-conscious investors limit valuation multiples and tightening regulations could restrict offshore drilling activity over time.
key_catalysts: Backfilling H2 2026 open capacity at stable or improving dayrates would significantly de-risk revenue guidance and could drive EPS above consensus -$0.11 expectation
Saudi Arabia and Mexico jack-up demand inflection supporting utilization and dayrate increases through 2027, driven by multi-year underinvestment in global offshore production capacity
Deleveraging through free cash flow generation as fleet utilization normalizes, reducing the $2.31B debt burden and improving net margins from the current 3.13% level
key_risks: Altman Z-Score of 0.37 and $2.31B net debt on a $1.3B market cap create material financial distress risk if dayrates decline or fleet utilization drops below breakeven levels
H2 2026 contract coverage at only 48% leaves approximately half of fleet capacity unbooked, exposing revenue to dayrate and demand volatility in a potentially softening market
Oil price decline below $60/bbl or recession-driven demand destruction could compress jack-up dayrates 20-30%, devastating 3.13% profit margins and ability to service debt obligations
sources: Borr Drilling Q1 2026 Earnings Release and Q4 2025 Annual Results — SEC Form 6-K, $247M Q1 revenue, $1.06B FY2026 guidance, Noble acquisition, February/May 2026
